---
title: "Executive Summary: Microstructure Factor Zoo"
subtitle: "15 L2 Features → 1-Minute AAPL Returns | MGMTMFE 412 — Market Frictions"
author: "UCLA Anderson School of Management — Spring 2025"
date: today
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    geometry: "margin=1.1in, top=0.9in, bottom=0.9in"
    fontsize: 11pt
    number-sections: false
    colorlinks: true
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \usepackage{booktabs}
        \usepackage{float}
---

## Intuition

Every second, the AAPL order book contains a snapshot of every visible buy and sell
order at 10 price levels. That snapshot encodes information: the *balance* between
buy-side and sell-side pressure, the *shape* of the book (thin top vs deep support),
the *pace* of trades being executed, and the *recent drift* in the weighted mid-price.

If any of these signals predict where the price will be 60 seconds from now, a trader
who can read them faster than the market can extract small but consistent profits —
**on every second of every trading day**.

The question is not whether the signals exist. They do. The question is whether the
profit from acting on them survives the cost of acting on them.

---

## The 15 Features

We construct features in three families:

**Order flow imbalance (OFI):** The ratio of bid-side to ask-side volume at the best
quote, measured instantly and with 5s and 30s rolling averages. A buy-side skew
(more willing buyers than sellers at the best prices) predicts near-term price increases.

**Book shape:** How the order book is *structured* across price levels — whether
quantity increases or decreases as you move away from the best quote, and whether
the top of the book is thin relative to deeper levels. A thin book amplifies price
impact; a steep book slope signals liquidity fragility.

**Price and trade dynamics:** The microprice (weighted fair-value mid between bid and
ask), its recent drift, realized short-term volatility, the arrival rate of trades,
the net direction of executed flow (buyers hitting ask vs sellers hitting bid), and
the rate at which the best-level queue is being consumed.

---

## Results

The linear model (Ridge regression) gives the honest answer: **IC = 0.045**, stable
in both training and out-of-sample periods. An IC of 4.5% means the model predicts
the direction of the 1-minute return correctly about 52–53% of the time. That is real
predictive content — just barely above the 50% random baseline.

| Model | Train IC | Test IC | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ridge (OLS) | 0.045 | 0.046 | Real signal, weak and stable |
| LightGBM | 0.501 | 0.025 | Severe overfitting, no OOS value |

**The machine learning failure is itself a finding.** LightGBM (500 trees) appears to
achieve IC = 0.50 in-sample — 10× better than OLS. But on the out-of-sample test set,
it collapses to IC = 0.02: worse than the linear model. This is textbook overfitting:
with 414,000 training bars and essentially zero signal-to-noise in the label, gradient
boosting memorizes noise patterns. Reliable HFT machine learning requires years of data
and domain-constrained model architectures, not off-the-shelf ensembles.

---

## Which Features Matter

Ablation analysis (removing one feature at a time from the best model) identifies the
three dominant signals:

1. **Realized volatility:** High recent volatility predicts more volatile future returns —
   not the direction, but the magnitude. This is the classic volatility-clustering effect.

2. **Signed trade imbalance:** Net buying pressure over the last 30 seconds predicts
   upward price continuation. This is the microstructure analogue of momentum.

3. **30s OFI:** The medium-term book skew carries more directional information than
   the instantaneous snapshot — consistent with research showing that order book
   signals are more predictive when aggregated over time.

---

## The Friction That Ends the Story

The gross IC of 0.045 is real. But converting it into profit requires trading — and
trading requires crossing the bid-ask spread. For AAPL in early 2024, the average
half-spread is \$0.0046 per share. With \$50K notional (~270 shares), one round-trip
costs \$2.50 in TC. The gross PnL per trade from a 0.045 IC model is roughly
$\$0.13$ per trade. Transaction costs are **19× the gross signal**.

This is an even worse ratio than the SPY lead-lag strategy (9× for AAPL there).
The reason: the factor zoo model fires at 1-second bars, where microprice movements
in 60 seconds are much smaller than the spread on average. The signal is real but
the cost of access is not.

> **The bid-ask spread is the universal gatekeeper.** Whether the opportunity is
> a lead-lag correlation, an FOMC announcement overshoot, or a book-imbalance signal,
> the spread screens out all participants except those for whom it is a revenue source.
> The market is not inefficient — it is *efficiently protected by friction*.

---

## Connection to Market Frictions

All three strategies in this project confirm the same economic fact from different angles:

- **Lead-lag:** SPY predicts AAPL with IC 0.024. TC is 9× the gross signal.
- **FOMC fade:** Pre-announcement spread widening creates AND limits the opportunity.
- **Factor zoo:** 15 L2 features produce IC 0.045. TC is 19× the gross signal.

The common thread is that market frictions — specifically, the bid-ask spread arising
from adverse-selection between informed and uninformed traders — are not incidental
costs. They are the mechanism by which the market allocates its inefficiencies to the
participants best positioned to exploit them.
